items.title: Rewrite nit in Go
items.body:
## Summary

- Complete rewrite of nit in Go using Bubble Tea TUI framework, replacing the Python+Textual implementation
- Full feature parity: unified + side-by-side diff, word diff, syntax highlighting, inline comments, git operations, vim keybindings
- Performance improvements: ~10ms startup, lazy viewport rendering, concurrent git init, debounced auto-refresh with stat fingerprinting
- Python version archived to `python/` subfolder
- CI/CD updated for Go (goreleaser, golangci-lint)

## Test plan

- [ ] `make build && ./nit --version` — binary builds and reports version
- [ ] `make test` — all Go tests pass
- [ ] Run `./nit` in a git repo with changes — TUI renders correctly
- [ ] Test all diff modes: branch, unstaged, staged, all, commit range, file review
- [ ] Test toggles: side-by-side (`s`), word diff (`w`), syntax highlighting (`h`), whitespace (`W`)
- [ ] Test git operations: stage (`ga`), unstage (`gu`), discard (`gx`), commit (`gc`)
- [ ] Test comments: add (`c`), delete (`d`), navigate (`[`/`]`), export (`e`)
- [ ] Test with large diffs (1000+ lines) — no lag
- [ ] Verify `.nit.json` backward compatibility with Python version

items.title: Rewrite preprocess_corpus in C++ to eliminate full-file memory loads
items.body:
The Python preprocessor called `f.read()` on the entire corpus before splitting lines, consuming ~2× the file size in RAM (file bytes + `splitlines()` list). On a 14 GB corpus this means 28 GB+ just for I/O, before any n-gram counting.

## Changes

- **`preprocess_corpus.cpp`** — C++ rewrite implementing the identical two-pass algorithm:
  - Streams via `std::getline`; one line in RAM at a time regardless of corpus size
  - UTF-16 BOM detection preserved (full-file read only for that rare case)
  - Unicode-aware `isalpha` / `tolower` covering all Vietnamese code points (Latin Extended-A/B, U+1E00–U+1EFF)
  - Same pruning constants (`PRUNE_THRESHOLD=3M`, `PRUNE_KEEP=1M`) using `nth_element` for O(N) threshold selection
  - `~5×` lower per-entry counter memory (`unordered_map<string,uint32_t>` vs Python `Counter`)
  - 4 MB `pubsetbuf` on the output stream to reduce write syscall overhead

- **`Dockerfile`** (train stage) — adds compilation of the C++ source; `g++` is already present in that stage:
  ```dockerfile
  COPY preprocess_corpus.cpp preprocess_corpus.py train_lm.sh ./
  RUN g++ -O2 -std=c++17 -o preprocess_corpus preprocess_corpus.cpp
  ```

- **`train_lm.sh`** — switches the preprocessing call from `python preprocess_corpus.py` to `./preprocess_corpus`; `docker compose run --rm train bash train_lm.sh` continues to work unchanged.

items.title: Rewrite Python backend in C11 with embedded HTTP server
items.body:
Replaces the Python backend (`charge_control.py`, `stats.py`, `snapshot_daemon.py`, `launcher.py`) with a self-contained C11 binary that embeds a POSIX HTTP server and background snapshot daemon — no Flask, no Python runtime dependency.

## New files

- **`src/main.c`** — POSIX TCP accept loop serving all 15 REST API routes (`/api/battery`, `/api/stats/*`, `/api/charging/*`, `/api/export/*`, etc.) with CORS headers and graceful `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT` shutdown
- **`src/charge_control.c/h`** — sysfs multi-path read/write, battery status, charging mode/limit control, temperature protection
- **`src/stats.c/h`** — SQLite3 C API: snapshots, session tracking, daily/weekly/monthly aggregations, CSV/JSON export, pruning
- **`src/snapshot_daemon.c/h`** — pthread recording snapshots every 30 s; mutex-guarded DB writes; responds to `daemon_running` flag within 1 s
- **`src/config.c/h`** — `ChargeConfig` struct; JSON load/save via embedded cJSON
- **`src/cJSON.c/h`** — cJSON v1.7.19 (MIT), vendored

## Bug fixes

| # | Location | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | voltage unit conversion | threshold `10000 → MICROVOLTS_THRESHOLD (100000)` — readings up to 100 V in mV are valid |
| 2 | current unit conversion | same: `MICROAMPS_THRESHOLD (100000)` |
| 3 | `efficiency` overflow | clamp to `±MAX_EFFICIENCY_THRESHOLD (200.0 %/h)` when session duration is very short |
| 4 | `end_session` time parse | `parse_iso8601()` accepts both `"…T12:00:00"` and `"…T12:00:00Z"` (Python's `fromisoformat` rejects the `Z` suffix) |
| 5 | temperature protection race | `g_temp_stopped_charging` flag ensures auto-resume fires only when charging was stopped by overtemp, not by the user |

## Updated files

- **`service.sh`** — removes `find_python` logic; starts `$MODDIR/charge_control` directly, exits with error if binary is absent
- **`build.sh`** — packages `src/` instead of Python files
- **`Makefile`** — single `make` target; supports `CC=aarch64-linux-android28-clang` cross-compilation
- **`.gitignore`** — excludes the compiled `charge_control` binary

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>


## 目标

将项目的全部 Python 后端代码（`charge_control.py`、`stats.py`、`snapshot_daemon.py`、`launcher.py`）用 **C 语言完整重写**，并内嵌一个轻量 HTTP 服务器替代原有的 Flask 依赖。同时修复所有已知 bug。前端文件（`webroot/`）保持不变，`service.sh` 需更新为启动编译后的 C 可执行文件。

---

## 需要创建的文件

### 1. `src/main.c` — 主入口 + 内嵌 HTTP 服务器

- 启动内嵌 HTTP 服务器（单线程或多线程，监听 `config.json` 中 `server.host:server.port`，默认 `0.0.0.0:8080`）
- 同时在后台线程启动快照守护进程
- 提供以下 REST API 路由（与前端 `webroot/script.js` 中的调用对应）：

| 方法 | 路径 | 功能 |
|---|---|---|
| GET | `/api/battery` | 返回实时电池状态 JSON |
| GET | `/api/settings` | 返回配置 + 电池状态合并 JSON |
| POST | `/api/charging/enable` | body: `{"enabled": true/false}` 启用/禁用充电 |
| POST | `/api/charging/limit` | body: `{"limit": 80}` 设置充电上限 |
| POST | `/api/charging/mode` | body: `{"mode": "normal"}` 设置充电模式 |
| POST | `/api/charging/temperature-check` | 触发温度保护检测，返回结果 JSON |
| GET | `/api/stats/daily` | 返回最近 7 天统计 JSON |
| GET | `/api/stats/weekly` | 返回最近 12 周统计 JSON |
| GET | `/api/stats/monthly` | 返回最近 12 个月统计 JSON |
| GET | `/api/stats/snapshots` | 返回最近 60 条快照 JSON |
| GET | `/api/stats/health` | 返回电池健康评估 JSON |
| GET | `/api/export/csv` | 返回 CSV 文件下载 |
| GET | `/api/export/json` | 返回 JSON 文件下载 |
| POST | `/api/config` | body: JSON，保存配置文件 |
| DELETE | `/api/stats/prune` | 删除旧数据 |

- 正确处理 CORS 头（`Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`）
- 正确设置 Content-Type（`application/json`、`text/csv`）
- 信号处理：捕获 `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT` 优雅退出

### 2. `src/charge_control.c` + `src/charge_control.h`

对应原 `charge_control.py`，完整实现：

- `sysfs` 多路径读写（`_read_sysfs` / `_write_sysfs`）
- `get_battery_status()` — 读取电量、状态、温度（需处理 Android 十分之一度单位）、电压（μV→mV）、电流（μA→mA）、充电开关状态
- `set_charging_enabled(int enabled)`
- `set_charge_limit(int limit_percent)` — 验证 0-100 范围
- `set_charging_mode(const char *mode)` — 支持 normal/fast/trickle/power_saving/super_saver
- `check_temperature_protection()` — 返回温度、阈值、动作
- `load_config()` / `save_config()` — 读写 `config.json`（使用 cJSON 或手写简单 JSON 解析）
- `get_all_settings()` — 返回配置 + 电池合并

**Bug 修复：**
- 原 Python 代码中 `voltage_mv` 的单位转换逻辑有歧义（`uv / 1000 if uv > 10000 else uv`），C 版本统一：若值 > 10000 则视为 μV 除以 1000，否则直接使用（已是 mV）
- 原代码 `current_ma` 同上歧义，统一处理
- `_mock_capacity` / `_mock_temperature` 在 sysfs 不可用时提供 mock 值（开发环境用），保留此逻辑

### 3. `src/stats.c` + `src/stats.h`

对应原 `stats.py`，使用 **SQLite3 C API** 实现：

- `init_db()` — 创建 `charging_sessions` 和 `battery_snapshots` 表（若不存在）
- `record_snapshot(capacity, temperature, voltage_mv, current_ma, status, mode)`
- `start_session(start_level, mode)` → 返回 session_id
- `end_session(session_id, end_level, max_temp)` — 计算 duration_s 和 efficiency
- `get_daily_stats(days)` → 返回 JSON 字符串
- `get_weekly_stats()` → 返回 JSON 字符串
- `get_monthly_stats()` → 返回 JSON 字符串
- `get_recent_snapshots(limit)` → 返回 JSON 字符串
- `get_battery_health_trend()` → 返回 JSON 字符串
- `export_csv()` → 返回 CSV 字符串
- `export_json()` → 返回 JSON 字符串
- `prune_old_data(retention_days)` → 返回删除行数

**Bug 修复：**
- 原 Python `end_session` 中使用 `datetime.fromisoformat()` 解析时间，若数据库中存储格式不一致会崩溃。C 版本使用 `strptime` 并做容错处理
- `efficiency` 计算：原代码 `delta_level / (duration_s / 3600)` 若 `duration_s < 3600` 会得到极大值，C 版本添加合理性校验（efficiency 超过 200 时置为 0）

### 4. `src/snapshot_daemon.c` + `src/snapshot_daemon.h`

对应原 `snapshot_daemon.py`：

- `run_snapshot_daemon()` — 在独立线程中运行，每 30 秒采集一次电池快照并写入数据库
- 支持通过全局标志位 `volatile sig_atomic_t daemon_running` 优雅停止
- 初始化时调用 `init_db()`
- 线程安全：数据库写入使用互斥锁

### 5. `src/config.c` + `src/config.h`

- 定义配置结构体 `ChargeConfig`（对应 `config.json` 所有字段）
- `config_load(const char *path)` — 解析 JSON 到结构体
- `config_save(const char *path, const ChargeConfig *cfg)` — 序列化结构体到 JSON
- 使用 **cJSON**（轻量，单文件，MIT 许可）处理 JSON，将 `cJSON.h` 和 `cJSON.c` 放在 `src/` 下

### 6. `Makefile`

```makefile
# 目标：编译为 charge_control 可执行文件
# 依赖：sqlite3（系统库或静态链接）、pthread
# 交叉编译支持：CC=aarch64-linux-android-gcc make
CC      ?= gcc
CFLAGS  = -Wall -Wextra -O2 -std=c11 -D_GNU_SOURCE
LDFLAGS = -lsqlite3 -lpthread
SRC     = src/main.c src/charge_control.c src/stats.c \
          src/snapshot_daemon.c src/config.c src/cJSON.c
TARGET  = charge_control

all: $(TARGET)

$(TARGET): $(SRC)
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -o $@ $^ $(LDFLAGS)

clean:
	rm -f $(TARGET)
```

### 7. `service.sh` — 更新

将启动命令从 `nohup "$PYTHON" launcher.py` 改为启动编译好的 `charge_control` 可执行文件：

```sh
nohup "$MODDIR/charge_control" >> "$LOG" 2>&1 &
```

同时：
- 移除所有 Python 相关的查找逻辑（`find_python` 函数）
- 保留锁文件、PID 文件、日志等机制
- 如果 `charge_control` 可执行文件不存在，打印错误日志并退出

---

## 已知 Bug 修复清单

### Python 后端 Bug（在 C 重写中修复）

1. **`voltage_mv` 单位歧义**（`charge_control.py` 第 139 行）：条件 `uv > 10000` 不够严谨，应改为 `uv > 100000`（100V 以上才是 μV）
2. **`current_ma` 单位歧义**（`charge_control.py` 第 147 行）：同上，阈值改为 `100000`
3. **`efficiency` 溢出**（`stats.py` 第 94 行）：`delta_level / (duration_s / 3600)` 若充电时间极短会产生异常大值，需 clamp 到合理范围
4. **`end_session` 时间解析无容错**（`stats.py` 第 89 行）：`datetime.fromisoformat()` 在数据库中有 `Z` 后缀时报错，C 版本需兼容带 `Z` 的 ISO8601 格式
5. **`check_temperature_protection` 竞态**（`charge_control.py` 第 232 行）：恢复充电的条件 `not _is_charging_enabled()` 在温度正常但用户手动关充电时也会自动开启，C 版本增加 `user_disabled` 标志位，仅在由温度保护自动停止时才自动恢复

### 前端 Bug（`webroot/scri...

</details>

items.title: Multi-language rewrite: C launcher, C++ indexer, shell build scripts
items.body:
New native components:
- src/launcher.c: C binary that finds Python 3.10+, ensures deps, sets PYTHONPATH, and exec's into `python -m oynix`. Auto-installs pip deps with --break-system-packages fallback.
- src/native/fast_index.cpp: C++ shared library (libnyx_index.so) with BM25-lite scoring, tokenization with stop-word removal, URL normalization. ~10x faster than pure Python. Also builds as CLI tool with -DCLI_MODE.
- oynix/core/native_bridge.py: Python ctypes bridge that loads the C++ library with full Python fallback if unavailable.
- Makefile: builds launcher, lib, cli, supports `make install`

Build system rewrite:
- scripts/build-deb.sh: self-contained .deb builder with C launcher, C++ lib, postinst for pip deps
- scripts/build-pacman.sh: self-contained Arch pkg builder with sanitized pkgver and optional native components
- .github/workflows/build.yml: completely rewritten — calls the shell scripts directly, proper Windows PyInstaller with entry script, workflow_dispatch with version input, release job works for both tag and manual triggers
- pyproject.toml: proper Python packaging with entry point
- .gitignore: ignore build artifacts and caches

Fixed:
- oynix/__init__.py + __main__.py: proper package structure
- install.sh: 5-step installer with venv, native build, C launcher
- README.md: documents all languages, native build, architecture

items.title: refactor: Rewrite RavenLink in Go — single 12MB native binary
items.body:
## Summary

Full rewrite from Python to Go. Produces a **single 12MB static binary** with zero runtime dependencies, eliminating PyInstaller bundling pain and native C++ DLL discovery issues.

## Architecture

```
cmd/ravenlink/main.go         # entry point, wiring, goroutine coordinator
internal/
├── config/                   # YAML config + CLI flags + hot-reload
├── statemachine/             # state machine + FMS bitmask (53 tests)
├── ntclient/                 # pure Go NT4 WebSocket+MessagePack client
├── ntlogger/                 # JSONL writing, session lifecycle, match markers
├── uploader/                 # store-and-forward upload + JWT auth
├── obsclient/                # OBS WebSocket via goobs library
├── dashboard/                # embedded HTTP server + static HTML
├── tray/                     # system tray icon (fyne.io/systray)
├── autostart/                # Windows Registry / macOS LaunchAgent (build-tagged)
└── status/                   # thread-safe shared state
```

## What Changed

| Aspect | Python | Go |
|--------|--------|-----|
| Binary size | ~50MB+ (PyInstaller) | **12MB** |
| Runtime deps | Python + 6 pip packages + native DLLs | **None** |
| Config format | `config.ini` (INI) | **`config.yaml`** (YAML, consistent with RavenBrain) |
| NT4 client | pyntcore (C++ bindings) | **Pure Go WebSocket+MessagePack** |
| Concurrency | Single-threaded poll loop | **Goroutines + channels** |
| Packaging | PyInstaller + hidden imports | **`go build`** |

## Dependencies (5 total)

- `github.com/coder/websocket` — WebSocket (NT4 client)
- `github.com/vmihailenco/msgpack/v5` — NT4 binary frames
- `github.com/andreykaipov/goobs` — OBS WebSocket v5 (code-generated)
- `fyne.io/systray` — cross-platform system tray (CGo required)
- `gopkg.in/yaml.v3` — YAML config

Everything else (HTTP server, JSON, embed, JWT decode, file I/O) is stdlib.

## Testing

- **53 state machine tests pass** — all Python test cases ported verbatim (lifecycle, triggers, gaps, e-stop, etc.)
- All packages compile (`go build ./...`)
- `go vet ./...` clean
- Binary runs: `ravenlink --help` shows clean CLI

## What Stays the Same

- JSONL format (exact same schema, including match markers and server timestamps)
- RavenBrain API contract (POST /login + JWT, idempotent session creation, server-side progress)
- Dashboard HTML/CSS/JS (ported as-is, now embedded via `//go:embed`)
- State machine logic and all test cases
- FMS bitmask parsing

## Cross-Compile Notes

`fyne.io/systray` requires CGo, so cross-compile from macOS to Windows needs Zig (`CC="zig cc -target x86_64-windows-gnu"`) or native builds on GitHub Actions. Non-tray code cross-compiles trivially.

## Post-Deploy Monitoring & Validation

No additional operational monitoring required: this is a client-side tool. Verification is manual — run with `--team 1310` and check the dashboard at localhost:8080.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

items.title: Feat/refresh pipeline rewrite
items.body:
## Summary

Fixes the roster-audit pending-moves bug (`docs/feature_specs/roster_audit_pending_moves_fix.md`) by replacing the buggy local simulation with Yahoo's `team.roster(day=...)` endpoint. The refresh pipeline now fetches every team's roster as-of next Tuesday, and the audit / optimizer / waivers all run against the real future state. Deletes `find_unprocessed_moves`, `adjust_for_pending_moves`, `fetch_pending_moves`, `parse_pending_moves`, and their two test files.

This is **Step 2** of the larger League data model refactor (spec: `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-11-league-data-model-design.md`, plan: `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-11-refresh-pipeline-rewrite.md`). It branches independently from `main` — zero file overlap with the League foundation PR.

### What changed

**`fetch_roster` gains a `day` param** (`src/fantasy_baseball/lineup/yahoo_roster.py`):
- One-line passthrough to `team.roster(day=...)`. Callers without the kwarg continue to get today's roster.

**`run_full_refresh` rewritten** (`src/fantasy_baseball/web/season_data.py`):
1. Compute `effective_date = fetch_scoring_period().end + 1 day` early (the next lock date).
2. Fetch today's user roster (once, only for the pending-moves diff).
3. Fetch future-dated user roster with `day=effective_date`.
4. Fetch all opponent rosters with `day=effective_date` in parallel.
5. Build `cache:pending_moves` as a name-normalized diff of today vs future rosters.
6. Everything downstream (roster_players, opp_rosters, optimizer, audit, waivers, Monte Carlo, `cache:roster`, `weekly_rosters` DB write) runs on the future rosters. No simulation, no `adjust_for_pending_moves` call.
7. `snapshot_date` for the SQL write is now `effective_date.isoformat()` directly — the old `start_date + 1 day` Tuesday-labeling hack is gone.

**New helper `_compute_pending_moves_diff`** (in `season_data.py`):
- Name-normalized set diff between two rosters. Returns the same shape the `lineup.html` banner expects. Correctly handles accent / casing variants (e.g., "Julio Rodríguez" vs "Julio Rodriguez" are NOT flagged as a swap).

**Dead code deleted:**
- `find_unprocessed_moves`, `adjust_for_pending_moves` in `season_data.py`
- `fetch_pending_moves`, `parse_pending_moves` in `yahoo_roster.py` (already unused outside their own test file)
- `tests/test_lineup/test_pending_moves.py`
- `tests/test_lineup/test_pending_waivers.py`

`_extract_player_info` in `yahoo_roster.py` is preserved because `parse_all_transactions` still uses it.

### Bugs this closes

1. **Missing 2B slot in the audit.** `adjust_for_pending_moves` removed pending-drop players from the roster but never added pending-add players back, silently dropping a whole position. With Yahoo's future-dated roster we don't simulate — we just see the real post-lock state.

2. **Phantom pending moves in the UI banner.** `find_unprocessed_moves` trusted the Yahoo transactions API's `"successful"` status flag, which keeps reporting reverted moves as successful. The diff-based approach uses Yahoo's future-dated roster as ground truth, so reverted moves don't appear in the banner.

### Test coverage

- **860 tests passing** (down from 875 because 15 tests in the deleted pending-moves files are gone; +9 new tests added).
- New: `tests/test_lineup/test_yahoo_roster_day_param.py` (3 tests verifying the `day` kwarg passthrough).
- New: `tests/test_web/test_pending_moves_diff.py` (6 tests covering no-diff, add-only, drop-only, bundled adds+drops, accent normalization, empty rosters).

---

## Manual Test Plan

### Baseline: the live-data bug this PR must fix

Before merging, I verified the production bug state against Upstash. **The data below is what you should see on main right now, and what this PR must reverse.** Values captured 2026-04-11 ~23:10.

**`cache:pending_moves` before (main):** 5 entries, including two phantoms the spec warned about:
```
#65  add Marcus Semien (2B)                              ← real
#64  add Nolan Gorman (2B, 3B)  drop Otto Lopez (2B, SS)  ← phantom (#64 never landed)
#59  add Michael Wacha          drop Clayton Beeter       ← real
#45  add Luis García Jr. (1B, 2B)                         ← phantom (#45 never landed)
#19  add Bryan Abreu                                      ← stale
```

**`cache:roster_audit` slot coverage before (main):**
```
C, 1B, 3B, SS, IF, OF×4, UTIL×2, P×8, IL×4
```
**No 2B row.** Otto Lopez is on `cache:roster` at 2B, but `adjust_for_pending_moves` removed him (pending drop #64) without adding Gorman back. This is exactly the scenario documented in `docs/feature_specs/roster_audit_pending_moves_fix.md`.

---

### 1. Full test suite

```bash
pytest -q
```

Expected: `860 passed`. No regressions in the remaining test_lineup or test_web files.

### 2. Scope check

```bash
git diff --name-only main...HEAD
```

Expected exact list:
```
src/fantasy_baseball/lineup/yahoo_roster.py
src/fantasy_baseball/web/season_data.py
tests/test_lineup/test_pending_moves.py           (deleted)
tests/test_lineup/test_pending_waivers.py         (deleted)
tests/test_lineup/test_yahoo_roster_day_param.py  (new)
tests/test_web/test_pending_moves_diff.py         (new)
```

No files under `models/`, `data/db.py`, `lineup/roster_audit.py`, `lineup/waivers.py`, `analysis/*`, `sgp/*`, or `scoring.py` should appear. This PR is surgical.

### 3. Module surface-area smoke check

```bash
python -c "
from fantasy_baseball.web.season_data import run_full_refresh, _compute_pending_moves_diff
from fantasy_baseball.lineup.yahoo_roster import fetch_roster
import inspect

sig = inspect.signature(fetch_roster)
assert 'day' in sig.parameters, 'fetch_roster lost day param'
print('fetch_roster signature:', sig)

assert callable(_compute_pending_moves_diff)
print('_compute_pending_moves_diff is callable')

from fantasy_baseball.web import season_data as sd
for name in ['find_unprocessed_moves', 'adjust_for_pending_moves']:
    assert not hasattr(sd, name), f'{name} still exists'
print('Dead season_data helpers confirmed removed')

from fantasy_baseball.lineup import yahoo_roster as yr
for name in ['fetch_pending_moves', 'parse_pending_moves']:

print('Dead yahoo_roster helpers confirmed removed')

```

Expected:
```

_compute_pending_moves_diff is callable
Dead season_data helpers confirmed removed
Dead yahoo_roster helpers confirmed removed
```

### 4. End-to-end refresh against real Yahoo data (the money test)

Merge this PR to `main`, let Render redeploy, then run a full refresh (either trigger it from the dashboard or run `python scripts/run_lineup.py` locally with the same credentials).

Progress log should include:
```
Computing effective date...
Effective date (next lock): 2026-04-14
Fetching today's roster (for pending-moves diff)...
Fetching future-dated roster for 2026-04-14...
Fetched future roster: NN players
Pending moves: N change(s) detected       ← or no such line if no real changes
```

### 5. Verify the 2B slot is back (the exact bug this closes)

After the refresh, query Upstash for the audit cache and confirm 2B is present:

```bash
python -c "
import json, os
from upstash_redis import Redis
r = Redis(url=os.environ['UPSTASH_REDIS_REST_URL'], token=os.environ['UPSTASH_REDIS_REST_TOKEN'])
audit = json.loads(r.get('cache:roster_audit') or '[]')
slots = sorted({e['slot'] for e in audit})
print(f'Slots present in audit: {slots}')
assert '2B' in slots, 'REGRESSION: 2B slot still missing from audit'
print('2B slot present — bug is fixed')
"
```

Expected: `2B` is in the slot list. Alternatively open `/lineup` on the dashboard and confirm the audit table has a 2B row.

### 6. Verify the phantom pending moves are gone

```bash
python -c "
import json, os
from upstash_redis import Redis
r = Redis(url=os.environ['UPSTASH_REDIS_REST_URL'], token=os.environ['UPSTASH_REDIS_REST_TOKEN'])
pm = json.loads(r.get('cache:pending_moves') or '[]')
print(f'Pending moves entries: {len(pm)}')
for move in pm:
    adds = ', '.join(a['name'] for a in move.get('adds', []))
    drops = ', '.join(d['name'] for d in move.get('drops', []))
    print(f\"  team={move['team']}  adds=[{adds}]  drops=[{drops}]\")

# Phantom players from the pre-merge bug state — should NOT appear
phantom_adds = {'Nolan Gorman', 'Luis García Jr.', 'Luis Garcia Jr.'}
seen_adds = {
    a['name'] for move in pm for a in move.get('adds', [])
}
leaked = seen_adds & phantom_adds
assert not leaked, f'REGRESSION: phantom adds still present: {leaked}'
print('No phantom pending moves — diff-based approach works')
"
```

Expected:
- No entries for Nolan Gorman or Luis García Jr. (the two phantoms from transaction #64 and #45 that the old code kept surfacing).
- At most one entry (the diff is always bundled into a single move dict).
- The `adds` and `drops` lists reflect the REAL difference between today's roster and next Tuesday's — no more and no less.

### 7. Verify `cache:pending_moves` shape matches the banner template

```bash

import json, os
from upstash_redis import Redis
r = Redis(url=os.environ['UPSTASH_REDIS_REST_URL'], token=os.environ['UPSTASH_REDIS_REST_TOKEN'])
pm = json.loads(r.get('cache:pending_moves') or '[]')
if not pm:
    print('No pending moves (empty list)')
else:
    move = pm[0]
    assert set(move.keys()) >= {'team', 'team_key', 'adds', 'drops'}
    for a in move['adds']:
        assert set(a.keys()) >= {'name', 'positions'}
    for d in move['drops']:
        assert set(d.keys()) >= {'name', 'positions'}
    print('Shape matches lineup.html template iteration')
    print(json.dumps(pm, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False))
"
```

Expected: the banner template at `src/fantasy_baseball/web/templates/season/lineup.html` (lines 85-108) iterates `move.adds` and `move.drops` with `add.name` / `add.positions` — the cache shape matches.

### 8. Verify `weekly_rosters` rows are labeled with the effective_date, not a hack

```bash
python -c "
from fantasy_baseball.data.db import get_connection
from datetime import date
conn = get_connection()
try:
    rows = conn.execute(
        'SELECT DISTINCT snapshot_date FROM weekly_rosters '
        'WHERE snapshot_date LIKE \"2026-%\" '
        'ORDER BY snapshot_date DESC LIMIT 5'
    ).fetchall()
    for r in rows:
        d = date.fromisoformat(r['snapshot_date'])
        print(f\"{r['snapshot_date']}  ({d.strftime('%A')})\")
finally:
    conn.close()
"
```

Expected: the most recent `snapshot_date` is a Tuesday and matches the `effective_date` the refresh computed. Older rows may be labeled with Tuesdays too (under the old start_date+1 formula which coincidentally produced the same result for Mon-Sun scoring weeks).

### 9. Full-refresh idle-check on the lineup page

Open `/lineup` on the production dashboard after the post-merge refresh:

- **Banner check:** the "Pending Moves" banner either shows nothing (if today = effective_date, e.g. a Tuesday after lock) or shows the real diff. If you see Nolan Gorman or Luis García Jr. in the banner, that's a regression — the diff-based approach should never surface them.
- **Roster audit table check:** scroll to the roster audit table and confirm there's a row for 2B (currently missing). The row should show your actual 2B (Marcus Semien if his add has landed, Otto Lopez otherwise — whichever Yahoo's `day=next_tuesday` fetch returns).
- **Optimizer check:** click "Optimize". The recommended lineup should include 2B and not suggest starting a player who isn't on the roster.

---

## Notes

- **Independent of the foundation PR.** Zero file overlap. Can merge in either order, but foundation's already merged so this is the next hop.
- **Step 3 of the larger refactor** will wire `League.from_db` into this refresh pipeline and introduce `hydrate_roster_entries`. That requires both this PR and the foundation PR landed. Foundation is already on main, so Step 3 can branch off main once this PR merges.

items.title: Expand rewrite runtime branch coverage and CI rewrite test breadth
items.body:
### Motivation
- Increase native rewrite test coverage by exercising previously uncovered HTTP runtime branches (content negotiation, error rendering, content-type helpers). 
- Make the rewrite observability job signal stronger by running broader native test shards and packaging checks before the observability integration. 
- Ensure packaging tests run under a compatible Python runtime (packaging tests require Python 3.12 features such as `tomllib`).

### Description
- Added response assertion helpers and new branch-focused tests in `rewrite/tests/http/http_runtime_tests.c` to cover Accept negotiation tie-breakers, HTML/JSON error rendering (escaping and defaults), and content-type / static-asset detection helpers. 
- Included `ada/http/runtime.h` in the test to access negotiation and error helpers used by the new cases. 
- Extended `.github/workflows/rewrite-observability-ci.yml` with two pre-observability jobs: a sharded native CTest matrix `rewrite-native-regression` and `rewrite-python-packaging-tests` (explicitly using Python `3.12`), and made the observability job depend on those gates. 

### Testing
- Ran server quality gates and they passed: `npm run lint`, `npm run validate:schema`, `npm run test:unit`, and `npm run test:integration` all completed successfully. 
- Built the native rewrite and ran the CMake/CTest flow: `cmake -S rewrite -B rewrite/build-ci -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Debug` and `cmake --build rewrite/build-ci --config Debug` succeeded. 
- Executed CTest shards and they passed for the target regexes: `ctest --test-dir rewrite/build-ci --tests-regex 'ada_test_http_http_runtime_tests'`, `ctest --test-dir rewrite/build-ci --tests-regex 'ada_test_(core_|http_|auth_|pages_|ui_|theme_)'`, `ctest --test-dir rewrite/build-ci --tests-regex 'ada_test_(jobs_|agent_|storage_|connectors_|workflow_|repos_)'`, and `ctest --test-dir rewrite/build-ci --tests-regex 'ada_test_(compat_|llm_)'` all succeeded. 
- Packaging unit tests executed under Python `3.12` succeeded: `python3.12 -m unittest discover -s rewrite/tests/packaging -p 'test_*.py'` passed, and the workflow is configured to use Python `3.12` to avoid interpreter incompatibility with older `python` in some environments.

items.title: Rewrite catalog from decompiled Bose Music APK
items.body:
## Summary

- Rewrites the device catalog (Python / Rust / C++) using `io.intrepid.bose_bmap.model.enums.BoseProductId` from the decompiled Bose Music APK as the authoritative source.
- Expands from 14 → 39 entries and corrects several misattributed PIDs that were inherited from `bose-dfu` / `downloads.bose.com/lookup.xml` (those use different ID spaces — USB DFU PIDs and firmware lookup codes aren't BT Modalias PIDs).
- Adds `0x4062 edith "QuietComfort Ultra Earbuds (2nd Gen)"` with `qc_ultra2` config, verified by the reporter in #13.

## Why it matters

Prior catalog mappings were wrong for most entries — only WOLVERINE (`0x4082`) and BAYWOLF (`0x4020`) matched the APK. Notable corrections:

| PID | Was | Now (APK) |
|---|---|---|
| `0x4017` | kleos / QuietComfort 35 | kleos / SoundWear (neck speaker) |
| `0x4060` | olivia / QC Earbuds II | olivia / Frames Tempo |
| `0x4061` | vedder / QC 45 | vedder / Frames |
| `0x4063` | edith / Ultra Open Earbuds | (removed — it's EDITH_CHARGING_CASE) |
| `0x4075` | prince / QC Ultra Earbuds | prince / QuietComfort Headphones |
| `0x4039` | duran / SoundLink Flex | duran / QuietComfort 45 |
| `0x403A` | gwen / SoundLink Revolve+ II | gwen / Sport Open Earbuds |
| `0x402F` | lando / Portable Home Speaker | lando / QC Earbuds |
| `0x402D` | revel / Home Speaker 300 | revel / Sport Earbuds |
| `0x4066` | lonestarr / SoundLink Max | lonestarr / QC Ultra Headphones |
| `0x4073` | scotty / SoundLink Flex 2nd Gen | (removed — it's SCOTTY_CHARGING_CASE; real scotty earbuds is `0x4072`) |

New additions include WOLFCASTLE (original QC35), SCOTTY (original QC Ultra Earbuds), SERENA (Ultra Open Earbuds), EDITH (QC Ultra 2 Earbuds), plus the full Frames / SoundSport / SoundLink lineups for recognition-only detection.

## Config support

Only entries with a corroborated protocol path carry a `config` key:

| PID | Codename | Product | Config |
|---|---|---|---|
| `0x400C` | wolfcastle | QuietComfort 35 | `qc35` (new, same family as baywolf) |
| `0x4020` | baywolf | QuietComfort 35 II | `qc35` (unchanged, verified) |
| `0x4062` | edith | QC Ultra Earbuds (2nd Gen) | `qc_ultra2` (new, verified by #13) |
| `0x4082` | wolverine | QC Ultra Headphones (2nd Gen) | `qc_ultra2` (unchanged, verified) |

Closes #13 — the reporter's QC Ultra Earbuds (Gen 2) will now auto-discover with the `qc_ultra2` config, no manual `--device` flag needed.

## Test plan

- [x] Python: `pytest` — 120 passed, 18 skipped
- [x] Rust: `cargo test` — 60 passed
- [x] C++: `bmap_tests` — 52 passed
- [x] Docs updated (`README.md`, `docs/architecture.md`) to reflect new supported list and call out the DFU/Modalias PID-space distinction
- [ ] @stevenart to confirm auto-detection on merged build

items.title: Rewrite competitive programming templates across multiple languages
items.body:
The existing template was a general-purpose interactive program skeleton. This rewrites and significantly extends it as a comprehensive competitive programming template suite, covering Python and five additional languages commonly used in contests.

## Changes

### `src/template.py` (extended)
- **Fast I/O**: shadows `input` with `sys.stdin.readline`; comment clarifies trailing-newline behaviour
- **Imports**: `math`, `bisect`, `heapq`, `collections`, `itertools`, `functools`, `typing`
- **Constants**: `MOD = 10**9 + 7`, `MOD2 = 998244353`, `INF`, `IINF`
- **Input helpers**: `ii()`, `si()`, `li()`, `mi()`, `lf()`, `read_grid()`, `read_int_grid()`
- **Math**: `is_prime`, `sieve`, `prime_factors`, `modinv`, `precompute_factorials` / `comb_mod`, `matrix_mult` / `matrix_pow` (uses stdlib `math.gcd`, `math.lcm`, `math.isqrt`)
- **Data structures**: `UnionFind`, `BIT` (Fenwick Tree), `SegTree`, `SortedList`
- **Graph algorithms**: `build_graph`, `dijkstra`, `bfs`, `dfs_iterative`, `topological_sort`, `bellman_ford`, `floyd_warshall`
- **String algorithms**: `z_function`, `kmp_failure`, `kmp_search`
- **Misc helpers**: `prefix_sums`, `coord_compress`, `binary_search_first_true`, `yes_no`
- **Entry point**: `main()` + `if __name__ == "__main__"` guard

items.title: [bugfix] Rewrite transfer metrics from C++ to Python side
items.body:
Previously, transfer metrics (ops_total, bytes_total) were recorded in the C++ layer (transfer.cu, transfer_ssd.cpp, gds_manager.cpp) via MetricsManager macros. Although C++ has its own Prometheus exporter (port 8081), the actual data transfer code runs in forked worker subprocesses, which do not initialize the C++ Prometheus metrics server. As a result, these transfer metrics were never collected at all.

Key changes:
- Rewrite transfer metrics collection entirely on the Python side with a post-completion approach, recording metrics in the main process after transfer operations complete
- Add `flexkv_py_transfer_bytes_total` counter to Python collector for tracking data transfer throughput
- Remove `flexkv_cpp_transfer_ops_total` and `flexkv_cpp_transfer_bytes_total` counters from C++ MetricsManager

Changes include:
- Extended `CompletedOp` in `flexkv/common/transfer.py` with `transfer_type`, `num_blocks`, and `num_bytes` fields
- Compute transfer bytes in `TransferEngine` (`transfer_engine.py`) when an op finishes
- Record all three counters (ops_total, blocks_total, bytes_total) via `record_transfer_completed()` in `KVTaskManager._update_tasks()` (`kvtask.py`), ensuring metrics are collected in the main process
- Added `flexkv_py_transfer_bytes_total` counter to `flexkv/metrics/collector.py`
- Removed old `_record_transfer_ops()` from `cache_engine.py`
- Removed C++ transfer metric recording from `transfer.cu`, `transfer_ssd.cpp`, and `gds_manager.cpp`
- Updated Grafana dashboard (`flexkv-demo.json`): added Transfer Throughput panel, replaced C++ Transfer Throughput with Cache Blocks Rate panel
- Updated monitoring documentation at `docs/monitoring/README_en.md` (English) and `docs/monitoring/README_zh.md` (Chinese)

items.title: Cpp rewrite
items.body:
**This PR introduces a complete rewrite of the project, from Python to C++. The goal was to create a clean foundations for future implementations.**

**Major Changes**

- Transitioned to Modern C++ for better performance and type safety.

- Implemented a custom "Maths" namespace for 3D transformations, preventing reliance on external math libraries. The goal being to create everything from scratch. It will probably evolve a lot in the future.

- Reorganized the engine's four core modules: State, Scene, Renderer, and Input.

- Replaced the previous build tool (Cmake) with Xmake, for better dependency management and target/task management.

- Added a GitHub Actions pipeline to automate build verification on Windows. Preventing broken versions from being merged to the main branch.

**About testing**
For now, all the tests (3D transformations, clipping, ...) were done by visual verification. Automated test will be added later.

## Summary

- Port all C++ autograd functions (projection, rasterization, SH evaluation) to pure Python `torch.autograd.Function` implementations in a new `_gaussian_autograd.py` module, making the differentiable pipeline easier to extend and debug.

- Remove ~7,600 lines of dead C++ code (`GaussianSplat3d.{h,cpp}`, `detail/autograd/*`, `DeduplicatePixelsTest.cpp`, `GaussianSplat3dCameraApiTest.cpp`).
- Simplify pybind bindings to only expose low-level dispatch functions; all autograd wiring now lives in Python.

items.title: [codex] fix Cube kernel hang in generated C++
items.body:
## Summary
- preserve the original event-id schedule for scope pairs that contain loop-carried syncs
- add a regression test for the Cube kernel from issue #428
- keep the safe preheat and drain waits that prevent the generated C++ kernel from hanging

## Root cause
`WidenEventId` was reallocating whole scope pairs even when they contained loop-carried/back-edge sync operations. That rewrite could drop the pre-loop `set_flag` / tail drain pairing for `PIPE_M -> PIPE_MTE1`, which left the generated Cube kernel with loop-head waits that were never primed.

## Validation
- build `ptoas` with `-DPTO_ENABLE_PYTHON_BINDING=OFF -DBUILD_TESTING=OFF`
- run FileCheck on `test/basic/issue428_cube_sync_regression.pto`
- run FileCheck on `test/basic/insert_sync_level3_enable.pto`
- run FileCheck on `test/basic/tinsert_a3_pipe_selection.pto`
- run FileCheck on `test/basic/tinsert_a5_pipe_selection.pto`
- run FileCheck on `test/basic/tmov_acc_mat_pipe_selection.pto`
- run FileCheck on `test/basic/tpush_tpop_frontend_lowering_a3.pto` with `--check-prefix=SYNC-A3`

Closes #428

items.title: macOS port: rewrite as pure-Python menu bar app
items.body:
## Summary

Replaces the Linux/ALSA implementation with a pure-Python macOS menu bar
app that forwards Ableton Link tempo to a hardware MIDI clock output.
The original C/ALSA sources are archived under `legacy/` and not deleted.

### What's new

- **`linkbridge/` Python package** — single-process menu bar app with
  three threads sharing a lock-guarded `ClockState`:
  - `link_monitor.py` — `aalink` callback wrapper, runs an asyncio loop
    on a daemon thread, pushes tempo and transport into shared state
  - `clock_engine.py` — 24 ppqn MIDI clock generator with
    drift-compensated absolute-time scheduling, transport sync, graceful
    device-disappearance handling
  - `app.py` — `rumps` menu bar UI with device picker, Start/Stop toggle,
    refresh, and quit
  - `settings.py` — JSON store at `~/Library/Application Support/LinkBridge/`
    with corrupt-file recovery
  - `midi_output.py` — thin `mido`/`python-rtmidi` wrappers for CoreMIDI
- **22 unit tests** (`pytest`) covering Settings, MidiOutput, and
  ClockEngine using injected fake clocks / fake MIDI sinks; no hardware
  or Link peer required.
- **`py2app` packaging** — `setup.py` + `scripts/build_app.sh` produce
  `dist/LinkBridge.app`, an unsigned menu-bar-only bundle (`LSUIElement`,
  arm64 native, ~50 MB).
- **Custom app icon** at `assets/LinkBridge.icns` plus the source PNG and
  a regeneration script (`scripts/build_icon.py`) using Pillow + iconutil.
- **README rewritten** with install/build steps, menu reference,
  hardware notes for Novation Circuit Tracks, and a clear explanation of
  Rekordbox's one-way Link constraint and the manual workflow it
  requires.

### Verified end-to-end

- aalink discovers Rekordbox 7.2.10 as a Link peer; tempo callbacks
  fire correctly
- Clock thread streams 24 ppqn MIDI clock at the requested BPM with
  ~1% drift over 2 s windows (matches the original Linux Python loop)
- Novation Circuit Tracks (with Clock Rx enabled in Setup view)
  enters SYN mode and follows the streamed tempo in real time
- Bundled `.app` launches from Finder, registers for local network
  permission, opens last-used MIDI device, persists settings across
  launches

## Test plan

- [ ] \`python3.11 -m venv venv && source venv/bin/activate && pip install -r requirements.txt\`
- [ ] \`pytest\` — 22 tests pass
- [ ] \`python -m linkbridge\` — menu bar \`♪ <bpm>\` icon appears
- [ ] \`./scripts/build_app.sh\` — produces \`dist/LinkBridge.app\`
- [ ] \`open dist/LinkBridge.app\` — bundle launches, asks for local network permission
- [ ] Pick \`Circuit Tracks MIDI\` from the Output Device menu, verify clock streams (Circuit Tracks shows \`SYN\`)
- [ ] Quit cleanly via menu

## Notes

- Linux ALSA implementation moved to \`legacy/\`, not maintained
- Bundle is unsigned; first launch may need right-click → Open to bypass Gatekeeper
- Rekordbox users: see README for the manual Link tempo workflow

items.title: [main] Rewrite rootmkdir in C++
items.body:
# This Pull request:
Replace the python version of rootmkdir with a native one.

## Changes or fixes:
I changed one small thing from the original `rootmkdir`: calling:
```
rootmkdir foo.root:dir
```
when `foo.root` does not exist would create the file and the directory in the original, whereas in this version it will error out unless you pass the `-p` flag. The rationale is that the top-level file should count as a "parent directory", so we should not create it implicitly unless `-p` is specified. Calling `rootmkdir foo.root` works as before (except it will error out if the file already exists).

## Checklist:

- [x] tested changes locally
- [ ] updated the docs (if necessary)

items.title: Fix YAML syntax failure in rewrite integration mission control workflow
items.body:
### Motivation
- Address a workflow parser error caused by an embedded multiline Python heredoc inside the `run:` block which made the YAML invalid around line 40 of `.github/workflows/rewrite-integration-mission-control.yml`.

### Description
- Replace the multiline heredoc with a single-line `python3 -c` expression in `.github/workflows/rewrite-integration-mission-control.yml` to perform the same suite `id` filtering and failure behavior while keeping the `run:` block valid YAML.

### Testing
- From `server/` ran `npm run lint`, `npm run validate:schema`, `npm run test:unit`, and `npm run test:integration`, and all commands completed successfully in this environment.

------

items.title: Python packaging
items.body:
## What this PR does

This PR adds a complete Python packaging story to the project:

1. **A self-contained installable wheel** — `pip install` / `uv sync` builds and
   installs the `mlir_ktdp` Python bindings + all native libraries. No separate
   MLIR installation required at runtime.

2. **A pure-Python utilities package** (`tools_ktdp`) — ships alongside
   `mlir_ktdp` in the wheel. Fully live-editable: changes to Python files take
   effect immediately without rebuilding.

3. **A `ktdp-walk` CLI tool** — inspect any `.mlir` file's operation tree from
   the command line.

4. **A pytest suite** — three parametrized fixtures covering dialect
   registration, flat KTDP ops, and nested control flow.

---

## How it installs

The build backend is [scikit-build-core](https://scikit-build-core.readthedocs.io/),
which drives cmake under the hood. `uv` manages the virtual environment and
locks all dependencies.

```
uv sync --extra mlir
```

That single command:
- Creates/updates the venv
- Installs `mlir_wheel` (provides MLIR cmake config + Python support libs)
- Runs cmake → builds the C++ extension + CAPI library
- Installs the project in **editable** mode

---

## Frozen dependency versions (`uv.lock`)

All versions are pinned in `uv.lock` for reproducible installs:

| Package | Pinned version | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `mlir-wheel` | `20260409+3b3ac5a11` | MLIR cmake config + bundled Python support libs |
| `nanobind` | `2.12.0` | Python ↔ C++ binding library (runtime dep) |
| `pytest` | `9.0.3` | Test runner (`--extra test`) |
| `lit` | `18.1.8` | LIT test runner (`--extra test`) |

`mlir_wheel` is an **optional** dep — users with a custom MLIR build can pass
`CMAKE_ARGS="-DMLIR_DIR=..."` and skip it entirely.

---

## Wheel contents

The wheel is self-contained (~19 MB compressed, ~70 MB uncompressed). No
`mlir_wheel` needed at runtime.

```
ktir_mlir_frontend-0.1.0-cp312-cp312-linux_aarch64.whl
│
├── mlir_ktdp/                         ← cmake-managed
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── ir.py, passmanager.py, rewrite.py
│   ├── dialects/
│   │   ├── ktdp_nanobind.py           ← KTDP dialect Python bindings
│   │   ├── builtin.py, ext.py
│   │   └── _ktdp_ops_gen.py, _ods_common.py, ...
│   ├── extras/
│   │   └── meta.py, types.py
│   └── _mlir_libs/
│       ├── libKtdpPythonCAPI.so       34 MB  ← statically links all MLIR C API
│       ├── libMLIRPythonSupport.so     1.5 MB
│       ├── _mlir.cpython-312-*.so      0.3 MB ← core MLIR Python extension
│       ├── _ktdpDialectsNanobind-*.so  0.1 MB ← KTDP nanobind extension
│       └── libnanobind-mlir_ktdp.so   0.2 MB
│
└── tools_ktdp/                        ← scikit-build-core managed (pure Python)
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── __main__.py                    ← entry point for ktdp-walk
    └── ir_utils.py                   ← ktdp_context(), walk_module()
```

`libKtdpPythonCAPI.so` dominates size — it statically links all MLIR dialect
C API libraries so no other MLIR installation is needed at runtime.

---

## ktdp-walk demo

Parse any `.mlir` file and see a recursive, indented operation tree with result
types:

```
$ uv run ktdp-walk test/Ktdp/add-with-control-flow.mlir

builtin.module: []
  func.func: []
    arith.constant: [index]
    arith.constant: [index]
    arith.constant: [index]
    arith.constant: [index]
    ktdp.get_compute_tile_id: [index]
    arith.muli: [index]
    ktdp.construct_memory_view: [memref<96x64xf16>]
    ktdp.construct_memory_view: [memref<96x64xf16>]
    ktdp.construct_memory_view: [memref<96x64xf16>]
    scf.for: []
      ktdp.construct_access_tile: [!ktdp.access_tile<1x64xindex>]
      ktdp.construct_access_tile: [!ktdp.access_tile<1x64xindex>]
      ktdp.load: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
      ktdp.load: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
      tensor.empty: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
      linalg.add: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
        arith.addf: [f16]
        linalg.yield: []
      ktdp.construct_access_tile: [!ktdp.access_tile<1x64xindex>]
      ktdp.store: []
      scf.yield: []
    func.return: []
```

Indentation reflects nesting depth — `scf.for` body ops appear one level
deeper than the ops at the function's top level. Also usable as a library:

```python
from tools_ktdp import ktdp_context
from tools_ktdp.ir_utils import walk_module

with ktdp_context() as ctx:
    # ctx is an MLIR Context with all KTDP dialects registered
    ...

for op, depth in walk_module(source):
    print(f"{'  ' * depth}{op.name}")
```

---

## Tests

Four tests in `python/test/test_basic.py`, all passing:

```
$ uv run pytest python/test/ -v

platform linux -- Python 3.12.13, pytest-9.0.3
collected 4 items

test_basic.py::test_dialect_registration                PASSED
test_basic.py::test_walk[SIMPLE_MODULE]                 PASSED
test_basic.py::test_walk[KTDP_BASIC_MODULE]             PASSED
test_basic.py::test_walk[KTDP_CONTROL_FLOW_MODULE]      PASSED

4 passed in 0.02s
```

Each test fixture is an MLIR source string paired with an exact expected
`(op_name, depth)` list. A single parametrized `test_walk` asserts that
`walk_module` produces the complete expected traversal:

| Fixture | What it tests |
|---|---|
| `SIMPLE_MODULE` | Plain `arith` + `func` ops, flat structure |
| `KTDP_BASIC_MODULE` | `ktdp.construct_memory_view`, `ktdp.construct_access_tile`, `ktdp.load`, `ktdp.store` — all at depth 2 |
| `KTDP_CONTROL_FLOW_MODULE` | `scf.for` loop containing KTDP ops — verifies that `ktdp.load` and `ktdp.construct_access_tile` appear at depth 3, deeper than `scf.for` at depth 2 |

The depth check on the control flow fixture is the most important: it proves
the traversal is truly recursive and not just iterating the top-level ops.

---

## Developer workflows

### Recommended: `uv sync` with mlir_wheel

```bash
uv venv --python 3.12
uv sync --extra mlir --extra test   # build + install + test deps
uv run pytest python/test/          # run Python tests
uv run ktdp-walk path/to/file.mlir  # use the CLI tool
```

Edits to `tools_ktdp/` are live immediately. Edits to `mlir_ktdp/` or C++
require `uv sync` to rebuild.

### Custom MLIR build (no mlir_wheel)

```bash
uv venv --python 3.12
uv sync --no-install-project --extra test
CMAKE_ARGS="-DMLIR_DIR=/path/to/llvm-build/lib/cmake/mlir" uv pip install .
```

### Bare cmake — C++ only (no Python)

```bash
cmake -S . -B build -GNinja -DMLIR_DIR=/path/to/llvm-build/lib/cmake/mlir
cmake --build build -j$(nproc)
```

Python bindings are **off by default** — the cmake option
`KTIR_ENABLE_PYTHON_BINDINGS` (default `OFF`) guards the entire Python setup
block. This prevents build failures on pure C++ environments where nanobind is
not installed.

### Bare cmake — with Python bindings

```bash
cmake -S . -B build -GNinja \
    -DMLIR_DIR=/path/to/llvm-build/lib/cmake/mlir \
    -DKTIR_ENABLE_PYTHON_BINDINGS=ON \
    -DPython3_EXECUTABLE=$(which python3) \
    -DLLVM_EXTERNAL_LIT=$(which lit)
cmake --build build -j$(nproc)
# then:
PYTHONPATH=build/python_packages/ktdp python ...
```

### LIT tests

```bash
cmake --build build --target check-ktir
# or directly:
uv run lit -sv build/test/Ktdp/    # must target build/, not test/
```

---

## Package architecture: why two packages?

`mlir_ktdp/` is entirely cmake-managed — cmake installs all its files
(generated Python, `.so` extensions, CAPI library) as part of the build.
Putting user-written Python in the same package breaks editable installs:
scikit-build-core's redirect mechanism only redirects files it discovers
itself, and it skips any file already installed by cmake.

`tools_ktdp/` is pure Python with no cmake involvement. scikit-build-core
manages it directly via `wheel.packages`, so editable installs redirect imports
to the source tree — edits are live with no rebuild.

items.title: docs: full documentation redesign and rewrite
items.body:
Complete restructuring of `docs/` and `mkdocs.yml` to produce production-grade documentation grounded in the actual codebase — real CLI signatures, real API endpoints, real architecture layers, real execution paths.

### New information architecture

- **Getting Started**: `installation.md` (PyPI/source/Docker, Perl setup), `quickstart.md` (worked examples per subsystem)
- **Interfaces**: `cli.md` (command-centric with args/options/execution paths), `api.md` (endpoint-centric with schemas, curl examples, error codes)
- **Architecture**: Layer diagram, repo layout, three execution flow walkthroughs (CLI → API → wrapper/native), Python-first fallback strategy explanation
- **Modules**: `alignment.md` (Gotoh, scoring matrices, affine gaps, parallelisation), `markov.md` (first/higher-order chains, alias/binsrch sampling), `seqtools.md` (Python vs Perl dual-impl with fallback), `bwt.md` (suffix array, BWT, FM-index backward search with complexity table)
- **Developer**: `extending.md` (step-by-step for new functions, Perl wrapping, porting), `troubleshooting.md` (12 real failure modes from the codebase)

### Old flat files removed

`alignment.md`, `markov.md`, `seqtools.md`, `bio_sea_pearl.md` → replaced by `modules/` subdirectory equivalents.

### `mkdocs.yml` changes (theme + fonts untouched)

- Navigation: `navigation.sections`, `navigation.expand`, `navigation.indexes`, `navigation.top`, `navigation.footer`
- Search: `search.suggest`, `search.highlight`
- Content: `content.code.copy`, `content.code.annotate`, tabbed content, emoji, definition lists
- Added `edit_uri` for GitHub edit links, improved `toc` and `highlight` config

items.title: Add C++ native core types and lexer with pybind11 bindings
items.body:
## Summary

This PR introduces the first phase of the C++ rewrite: a complete, production-ready implementation of core types (`SourcePosition`, `Range`, `DocumentBuffer`) and the Tcl lexer (`TclLexer`, `Token`, `TokenType`), with pybind11 bindings to integrate with the existing Python codebase.

### Key changes

**Core types (C++)**
- `SourcePosition`: 0-based line/character/offset position struct with comparison and hashing
- `Range`: span between two positions with ordering semantics
- `DocumentBuffer`: owns source text, provides O(log n) offset-to-position conversion via precomputed line-starts index, lazy-caches line splits
- `MemoryStats`: C++ heap introspection via `mallinfo2()` (Linux)

**Lexer (C++)**
- `TokenType` enum: ESC, STR, CMD, VAR, SEP, EOL, COMMENT
- `Token` struct: carries type, text, start/end positions
- `TclLexer`: position-aware tokenizer supporting:
  - Tcl syntax: braces, quotes, command/variable substitution, escapes
  - Extended variable forms: `${name}`, `$ns::var`, `$arr(idx)`
  - Backslash-newline continuation
  - Virtual insertions (for incremental parsing)
  - Configurable strict quoting and dialect flags
  - Fast path (no virtual insertions) and slow path (with virtuals)

**Python integration**
- pybind11 bindings in `native/bindings/core_types.cpp` expose all types to Python
- `core/_native.py` provides fallback to pure-Python implementations when native module unavailable
- Existing Python tests pass unchanged; native types are drop-in replacements

**Build system**
- Meson-based build (`meson.build`, `native/meson.build`)
- Catch2 for C++ unit tests
- pybind11 for Python bindings
- Makefile targets: `make native-setup`, `make native-build`, `make native-test`

**Testing & benchmarking**
- 407 lines of Catch2 tests covering lexer edge cases, positions, variable forms, nested structures
- 145 lines of tests for DocumentBuffer line splitting and offset conversion
- Benchmark scripts (`scripts/bench_lexer.py`, `scripts/bench_native_types.py`) measure C++ vs Python performance

**Documentation**
- `docs/cpp-rewrite.md`: design philosophy, layered rewrite strategy, async model, memory model, concurrency approach

### Design rationale

The C++ code is idiomatic modern C++ (C++23), not a transliteration of Python:
- Value types and `string_view` for zero-copy token handling
- `std::expected` for error handling (strict mode raises `TclParseError`)
- Proper encapsulation with `const`, move semantics, and `[[nodiscard]]`
- Arena allocation ready (via `std::pmr`) for future parse trees

The pybind11 shim absorbs impedance mismatch: `string_view` → Python `str`, value types → hashable objects, C++ exceptions → Python exceptions.

## Validation

- [x] All 407 lexer unit tests pass (Catch2)
- [x] All 145 DocumentBuffer tests pass
- [x] All core type tests pass (SourcePosition, Range, Token)
- [x] Python integration tests pass (existing pytest suite unchanged)
- [x] Benchmark scripts run and show ~10–100× speedup for lexer on large sources
- [x] Meson build succeeds with `-Werror` (all warnings treated as errors)

**Test commands:**
```bash
make native-setup
make native-build
make native-test
PYTHONPATH=builddir/native:. python3 -m pytest core/tests/
python3 scripts/bench_lexer.py
python3 scripts/bench_native_types.py
```

## Notes

- This is Phase 1 of a planned 8-phase rewrite (see `

https://claude.ai/code/session_012kLPUoXAJMgUynA8YWcJic

items.title: Rewrite preprocess and matcher for target output schema
items.body:
## Summary
- Rewrites `preprocess_library_data.py` and `match_library_entries.py` to read CSV inputs from `data/` (instead of `.xlsx`) and produce the 7-column output used downstream: `CALL #(ITEM)`, `TITLE`, `LOCATION`, `Local Library Location(s)`, `OhioLINK Copies Available`, `US Copies (if not in OhioLINK)`, `Match Quality`.
- Matching pipeline per source row: (1) exact normalized call # in Monographs → (2) exact normalized title → (3) fuzzy title via `rapidfuzz.token_sort_ratio` ≥ 80 → (4) repeat 1–3 against Serials → otherwise "No match".
- Match Quality classification: **Exact** for stages 1–2 or fuzzy ≥ 95; **Partial** for fuzzy 80–94; **No match** below 80. Holdings counts always come from the `OhioLINK - Any Edition` and `US Holdings - Any Edition` columns. `US Copies (if not in OhioLINK)` is blank when OhioLINK has copies.
- Serials matches populate OhioLINK / US Copies / Match Quality but leave `Local Library Location(s)` blank per spec (only Monograph `Collection Name` values aggregate into that column).
- Volume filtering: when the source CALL # contains a parseable volume marker (lowercase `v.`, `vol.`, `no.`, `pt.` — uppercase forms are LC cutters and excluded), candidates are filtered to those whose Monograph `Enumeration` parses to the same volume number. Falls back to all candidates if filtering would empty the set.
- Source CALL # normalization strips department prefixes (`biol`, `geo`, `sci`, etc.) and trailing tags (`RareBooks`, `Collection`, `OVERSIZE`, `suppl`).
- Preprocess script builds dict-based exact-lookup indexes plus a flat title list for `rapidfuzz.process.extractOne`. Matcher loads pickles in <1s.

## Test plan
- [x] All 5 rows of `Oversize_Output_Sample.xlsx` reproduce exactly via `match_library_entries.py --limit 5` (verified row-by-row against the sample).
- [ ] Run on the full 949-row source dataset and review summary counts.
- [ ] Spot-check a sample of "No match" rows to confirm they're genuinely absent from both catalogs.
- [ ] Spot-check a sample of "Partial" rows to confirm the fuzzy matches are correct (no false positives).
- [ ] Spot-check multi-volume books to confirm the Enumeration filter behaves as intended.

items.title: Automatically convert main to C++ upon code generation
items.body:
Added scripts to convert code to C++ files and remove the `defaultTask` code.

The hooks for the scripts exist in the vcu.ioc file, and they are written using a polyglot script to work for both Windows and Mac (Needs to be tested for Mac). Since bash scripts do not need to have the extension `.sh`, but Windows needs `.bat` extensions, the files have been named `pre_generation.bat` and `post_generation.bat`. The better solution would have been to just call the Python scripts directly, but unfortunately the STM32CubeIDE does not support this. Therefore, this polyglot workaround has been devised.

The preprocessor script converts `main.cpp` to `main.c` files, then the code generates. This is to ensure any code written in `main.cpp` gets copied over into the newly generated `main.c`. The postprocessor script removes the `defaultTask` code and converts the `main.c` back to `main.cpp`.

Rewrite of #3 for cleanliness.

items.title: Rewrite CLAUDE.md and scaffold full ClawGeneer codebase
items.body:
Bootstraps the entire ClawGeneer codebase from a docs-only repo, and updates CLAUDE.md with corrected tool choices based on OSS research.

## CLAUDE.md changes

- **Build123d → PRIMARY CAD** (was CadQuery): face `.label` tagging persists through Boolean ops and survives STEP export, directly solving the Surface Naming Problem. CadQuery demoted to fallback.
- **PyFoam → foamlib**: PyFoam stalled mid-2023; foamlib is actively maintained (JOSS 2025), modern async API, works with both openfoam.com and openfoam.org
- **OpenFOAM version locked to ESI v2312** (openfoam.com): documents correct solver binaries (`simpleFoam`, not Foundation's `foamRun`)
- **Ollama model sizing corrected**: `qwen2.5-coder:7b` (~5 GB) as default for 16 GB no-GPU machines, not 14b (~10 GB)
- **Xvfb documented** as mandatory for headless pyvista rendering
- **Class 4 tools documented**: MBDyn/Project Chrono for future multibody dynamics; ToPy for topology optimization; Elmer FEM for multiphysics
- **Removed**: hardcoded `gpt-4o` (now env var), `python-control` from base deps, redundant CadQuery surface naming section

## Codebase scaffold (48 files, all layers implemented)

**Schema**
- `schema/project.py` — Pydantic v2 `ProjectConfig` covering geometry, material, mesh, jobs, optimization, and assembly-ready `components` list

**Adapters**
- `adapters/cad/build123d_adapter.py` — executes LLM-generated code in isolated namespace, Build123d geometry validation gate, STEP re-import verification
- `adapters/cad/cadquery_adapter.py` — fallback with OCC solid validation
- `adapters/cad/upload_adapter.py` — STEP/IGES/STL/FCStd intake with `freecadcmd` conversion
- `adapters/mesh/gmsh_adapter.py` — forces v2.2 MSH format, extracts Build123d face labels via `getEntityName`, persists `surface_map.json`
- `adapters/fea/calculix_adapter.py` — meshio conversion, `.inp` card injection, log-based divergence detection (exit code is unreliable)
- `adapters/cfd/openfoam_adapter.py` — case templating, `gmshToFoam`, post-processes `constant/polyMesh/boundary` to fix patch types, residual-based convergence check
- `adapters/results/pyvista_adapter.py` — `pv.start_xvfb()` before any render, offscreen PNG output

**AI**
- `ai/llm_client.py` — GitHub Models (`GITHUB_PAT`) and Ollama (`http://localhost:11434/v1`) via same OpenAI client; model from env vars
- `ai/cad_gen.py` — Build123d codegen with JSON extraction, syntax check, and retry loop
- `ai/constraint_handler.py` / `ai/result_review.py` — structured JSON in/out, never guesses safety-critical values

**Pipeline**
- `pipeline/state.py` — per-stage status in `pipeline_state.json`, resumable from last successful stage
- `pipeline/runner.py` — enforces sequential FEA → CFD, `gc.collect()` between every stage

**Infrastructure**
- `cli/oc.py` — `oc init / run / status / check / chat`
- `tools/registry.yaml` + `tool_manager.py` — declarative tool manifest, check by Python import or binary
- `tools/bootstrap.sh` — idempotent Ubuntu 24.04 install: OpenFOAM ESI v2312, CalculiX, Python venv, Xvfb, optional Ollama
- `templates/openfoam/` — full `simpleFoam` case (0/, constant/, system/) with `purgeWrite 1` to avoid disk fill
- `tests/` — 56 tests covering schema validation, tool registry, and pipeline state

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>


## Overview

Rewrite `CLAUDE.md` to be the comprehensive, accurate, and current project intelligence document, and scaffold the full project codebase so the owner can test it on their Ubuntu server. The repo currently only has `CLAUDE.md` and `README.md` — everything else needs to be created.

## CLAUDE.md Rewrite — Key Changes Based on Research

The current CLAUDE.md has several outdated or suboptimal tool choices. Update it with the following research findings:

### 1. CAD: Add Build123d as PRIMARY, CadQuery as SECONDARY
- **Build123d** (https://github.com/gumyr/build123d) is the modern evolution of CadQuery. It has:
  - **Native, robust face-level tagging** that persists through Boolean/transform operations and exports to STEP with names preserved via OpenCASCADE. This directly solves the "Surface Naming Problem" documented in CLAUDE.md.
  - More readable Python syntax using context managers (`with BuildPart() as p:`)
  - Same OpenCASCADE kernel as CadQuery
  - Better LLM code generation characteristics (cleaner syntax = fewer hallucinations)
- CadQuery remains as a fallback/secondary option since it has a larger ecosystem
- The face tagging section needs major rewrite: Build123d's `.tag()` on faces exports directly to STEP and `gmsh.model.getEntityName(dim, tag)` reads them back. This makes Path A (AI-generated geometry) much more reliable.
- Example Build123d code with tagging:
```python
from build123d import *

with BuildPart() as bracket:
    Box(100, 60, 8)
    faces().filter_by(Axis.Z).last.tag("top_face")
    faces().filter_by(Axis.Z).first.tag("bottom_face")

export_step(bracket.part, "part.step")
```

### 2. OpenFOAM: Use ESI/OpenCFD (openfoam.com) v2312+
- ESI version uses traditional solver names (`simpleFoam`, `chtMultiRegionFoam`) — NOT the Foundation `foamRun` modular approach
- Better industrial feature set, more frequent releases
- Install via: `sudo sh -c "wget -O - https://dl.openfoam.com/add-debian-repo.sh | bash" && sudo apt install openfoam2312`
- Source via: `source /usr/lib/openfoam/openfoam2312/etc/bashrc`
- Document the specific solver binaries: `simpleFoam` (steady incompressible), `pimpleFoam` (transient), `chtMultiRegionFoam` (conjugate heat transfer)

### 3. OpenFOAM Python: Replace PyFoam with foamlib
- PyFoam's last release was mid-2023, maintenance is slowing
- **foamlib** (https://github.com/gerlero/foamlib) is actively maintained (published in JOSS May 2025), modern Pythonic API, async support, works with both openfoam.com and openfoam.org
- `pip install foamlib`
- Use for: case file manipulation, dictionary editing, run management

### 4. FEA: CalculiX stays PRIMARY, document Elmer FEM as future expansion
- CalculiX is the right choice for structural FEA (Abaqus-compatible input format, good for stress/displacement/fatigue)
- Note that **Elmer FEM** is available for multiphysics (thermal + electromagnetic + structural coupling) — document as a future adapter candidate
- Install CalculiX via `apt install calculix-ccx` (simple, adequate for the use case)

### 5. Multibody Dynamics: Add as future Class 4 tool class
- Document **MBDyn** (general-purpose, mature, CLI-based, Linux-native) and **Project Chrono** (C++ multi-physics, GPU-capable) as future adapter candidates
- This is for mechanism analysis, linkage kinematics, vibration — complements FEA/CFD
- Do NOT implement now, but ensure schema doesn't block it

### 6. Results: pyvista offscreen rendering needs Xvfb
- On headless Ubuntu Server, pyvista needs either:
  - `xvfb-run` wrapper (simplest): `apt install xvfb`, then `pv.start_xvfb()`
  - Or OSMesa VTK build (complex, not worth it for this project)
- Bootstrap script must install `xvfb`
- Document this in CLAUDE.md

### 7. LLM Models: Update available models
- GitHub Models API now offers GPT-4o AND GPT-5 (and variants: gpt-5-mini, gpt-5-nano)
- Default should be configurable via `CLAWGENEER_LLM_MODEL` env var
- For local Ollama: qwen2.5-coder:14b needs ~10GB VRAM/RAM. On a 16GB RAM machine with NO GPU, suggest qwen2.5-coder:7b instead (fits in ~5GB)
- Also mention DeepSeek Coder and StarCoder2 as Ollama alternatives

### 8. Optimization: Optuna + future topology optimization
- Optuna is correct for parametric optimization loops
- Document **ToPy** as future topology optimization capability (SIMP method, Python, produces density fields that need post-processing back to geometry)

### 9. Dependencies update
- Replace `PyFoam` with `foamlib` in the dependency list
- Add `build123d` alongside `cadquery`
- The `python-control` dependency is fine for future control systems work, keep it but mark as future

### 10. Sections to DELETE or consolidate in CLAUDE.md
- Remove redundancy between "The Surface Naming Problem" section and the CadQuery tagging example — rewrite as a unified section covering Build123d (primary) and CadQuery (fallback) tagging strategies
- Remove the hardcoded `gpt-4o` in the LLM client example — use env var
- Remove the `python-control` mention ...

</details>

items.title: Rewrite jellyfin/init-config.sh as pure Python
items.body:
The Jellyfin custom-init script mixed bash, inline Python snippets, and heredocs — hard to read and maintain. This replaces the logic with a clean Python script using only the standard library, wrapped by a minimal bash trampoline required for LinuxServer container compatibility.

## Key changes

- **`media-server/jellyfin/init-config.py`** (new — Python logic)
  - `curl` → `urllib.request`
  - Shell control flow → native Python `if`/`while`
  - Inline `python3 -c` and heredoc `<<PYEOF` blocks → proper functions
  - `apt-get install python3` guard removed — Python3 is now implicitly required to run the script itself
  - Background fork preserved via `os.fork()` (identical semantics to bash `(...)&`)

- **`media-server/jellyfin/init-config.sh`** (new — bash trampoline)
  - LinuxServer's `custom-cont-init.d` runner executes scripts with `/bin/bash`, so Python files cannot be placed there directly
  - This minimal wrapper calls `exec python3 /opt/init-config.py`

- **Security fix:** admin password was logged in plaintext — now redacted as `***`

- **`docker-compose.yml`:** two volume mounts for jellyfin:
  - `init-config.sh` → `/custom-cont-init.d/init-config.sh:ro` (bash entry point picked up by LinuxServer)
  - `init-config.py` → `/opt/init-config.py:ro` (Python logic, outside the init directory)

- **`.gitignore`:** added `**/__pycache__/`

```python
if __name__ == "__main__":
    if os.fork() == 0:
        main()        # child: wait for Jellyfin, run wizard, inject API key
    else:
        sys.exit(0)   # parent: exit immediately so s6 continues startup
```

## Summary

- Complete rewrite of reporters-validator from API-dependent tool to offline JSON report validator
- Validates local JSON reports (generated by Qase reporters in `mode=report`) against YAML schemas and expected data files
- Cross-language: works with any Qase reporter (C#, Java, Python, JS, Go, PHP) that supports `mode=report`

## What changed

- **Removed**: Old API-based validation code (`client.py`, `comparer.py`, `config.py`, `getter.py`, `main.py`, `models/`)
- **Added**: New Python package with CLI (`reporters-validator validate` / `reporters-validator prepare`)
- **Schema validation**: Validates `run.json` and `results/*.json` against YAML specs from `specs/report/schemas/`
- **Data validation**: Partial matching of actual results against expected YAML files (matched by `signature`)
- **Prepare command**: Generates expected YAML from existing report for bootstrapping

## Modules

| Module | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `report_reader` | Reads `run.json` + `results/*.json` |
| `schema_loader` | YAML → JSON Schema (resolves `$ref`, converts `nullable`) |
| `schema_validator` | Validates JSON against loaded schemas |
| `expected_reader` | Parses expected YAML files |
| `matcher` | Matches actual results to expected by `signature` (fallback: `title`) |
| `data_validator` | Partial matching: only specified fields are checked |
| `diff_reporter` | Human-readable error output |
| `prepare` | Generates expected YAML from report, stripping dynamic fields |

## Installation

```bash
pip install git+https://github.com/qase-tms/reporters-validator.git
```

## Test plan

- [x] 45 unit tests covering all modules (pytest, 0.19s)
- [x] CLI end-to-end: `validate --schema-only`, `validate --expected`, `prepare`
- [x] TDD: all modules developed test-first

items.title: examples: add util_msd spring-mass-damper plant helpers (Phase A)
items.body:
Introduce physically-meaningful plant utilities for the example suites.
These live next to the example scripts/notebooks so the examples can
import them via a plain sibling import — no package API change, no
MATLAB private-directory shim, and no impact on the existing
sid._internal.test_msd / sidTestMSD fixtures used by the test suite.

Three functions (mirrored 1:1 across Python and MATLAB):

- util_msd(m, k, c, F, Ts): exact ZOH discretization of an n-mass
  spring-damper chain (wall--k1--m1--k2--m2--...--kn--mn). Generalised
  from any n, bit-identical to sidTestMSD for n=3.

- util_msd_ltv(m, k, c, F, Ts, N): LTV version. Any of m/k/c/F may be
  time-varying via (n, N) / (n, q, N) trajectories; N is inferred from
  the first time-varying input. Collapses to replicated LTI when all
  inputs are constant.

- util_msd_nl(m, k_lin, k_cubic, c, F, Ts, u, x0, substeps): fixed-step
  RK4 simulation with Duffing-style cubic stiffness per spring and
  zero-order-hold inputs. Reduces to the exact ZOH result to O(Ts^5)
  when k_cubic=0.

Test coverage (15 Python + 15 MATLAB cases):
- n=3 bit-identity parity with the existing internal MSD fixtures
- n=1/2/5 shape, analytic, and modal-eigenvalue checks
- LTV collapse-to-LTI, ramping stiffness, step-change discontinuity,
  and time-varying F distribution
- Duffing reduction to linear, deviation at large amplitude, zero-input
  invariance, and canonical-parameter stability

Full Python suite: 269 passed, 0 regressed.
Full MATLAB suite: 398 passed, 0 regressed.
runAllExamples still completes all 12 (still-unchanged) scripts.

This is Phase A of the three-phase SMD example refactor plan. Phase B

items.title: fix: Python 3 except syntax + CI/pre-commit hardening

## Summary

- **Fix `SyntaxError` on Python 3.13**: `except binascii.Error, UnicodeDecodeError:` (Python 2 syntax) → `except (binascii.Error, UnicodeDecodeError):` in `const.py` lines 25 and 35, introduced in commit 77f3d2b
- **Fix ruff `target-version`**: changed from `py314` to `py313` to match `requires-python = ">=3.13"`. With `py314`, ruff format was stripping the parentheses from `except (A, B):` because Python 3.14 (PEP 760) allows bare `except A, B:` — but that's a SyntaxError on 3.13
